```
julia> using IJulia

julia> notebook()
[ Info: running setenv(...)

# Single Ctrl+C

Process(setenv(..., ProcessSignaled(2))

julia> 
```

In 1.8-rc3 this doesn't work (tested on both `cmd.exe` and `powershell`):

```
julia> using IJulia

julia> notebook()
[ Info: running setenv(...)

# Single Ctrl+C

# Nothing happens

# Multiple Ctrl+Cs

WARNING: Force throwing a SIGINT
```
and the whole Julia session is dead.

version"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2022-09-05T10:30:27Z","updated_at":"2022-09-20T14:46:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Since Julia 1.8, a single Ctrl-C does not work, I always need to repeat Ctrl-C multiple times to get the forced SIGINT (with `WARNING: Force throwing a SIGINT`):


https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6933510/188428251-dec8a3da-7410-4ebf-b001-8de77457e14b.mov

I found this to be the case for anything that I interrupt, not just `sleep`. This means that, to exit Pluto, Jupyter, a web server or any other long running process, users will now need to **hold down** Ctrl-C, [printing multiple WARNINGs](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6933510/188687702-daf3e8f4-7bbc-447e-afde-7c9c03caca32.png), which makes Julia look unstable.

```
Julia Version 1.8.0
Commit 5544a0fab76 (2022-08-17 13:38 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (x86_64-apple-darwin21.4.0)
  CPU: 8 × Apple M1
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-13.0.1 (ORCJIT, westmere)
  Threads: 1 on 8 virtual cores
```
```
Julia Version 1.7.0
Commit 3bf9d17731 (2021-11-30 12:12 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (x86_64-apple-darwin19.5.0)
  CPU: Apple M1
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-12.0.1 (ORCJIT, westmere)
```

Same problem with the MacOS ARM build.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46635/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46635/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1252465788","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46635#issuecomment-1252465788","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46635","id":1252465788,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5Kpxx8","user":{"login":"fonsp","id":6933510,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY5MzM1MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6933510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp","html_url":"https://github.com/fonsp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-20T14:46:23Z","updated_at":"2022-09-20T14:46:23Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"It looks like it is still possible to interrupt Pkg precompilation with a single Ctrl+C ([video](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6933510/191289490-c45f635e-6483-4337-a14b-6d0e3177ac52.mov)), strange...

```julia
while true
	sleep(.1)
end
```


<details>
<summary>Details</summary>


```
julia> sleep(100)
^C^C^C^C^C^C^CWARNING: Force throwing a SIGINT
ERROR: InterruptException:

julia> while true
       sleep(100)
       end
^C^C^C^C^C^C^C^CWARNING: Force throwing a SIGINT
ERROR: InterruptException:

julia> while true
       sleep(.1)
       end
^CERROR: InterruptException:
Stacktrace:
 [1] poptask(W::Base.InvasiveLinkedListSynchronized{Task})
   @ Base ./task.jl:921
 [2] wait()
   @ Base ./task.jl:930
 [3] wait(c::Base.GenericCondition{Base.Threads.SpinLock})
   @ Base ./condition.jl:124
 [4] _trywait(t::Timer)
   @ Base ./asyncevent.jl:138
 [5] wait
   @ ./asyncevent.jl:155 [inlined]
 [6] sleep(sec::Float64)
   @ Base ./asyncevent.jl:240
 [7] top-level scope
   @ ./REPL[14]:2

julia> 
```

</details>


This means that Packages that use the "Press Ctrl+C to stop" UI, like an HTTP.jl server, can use this as a **workaround**.

## Example with HTTP.jl:

Instead of:

```julia
server = HTTP.serve!(...)

@info "Server running, press Ctrl+C to interrupt..."
wait(server)
```

You can use:

```julia
server = HTTP.serve!(...)

@info "Server running, press Ctrl+C to interrupt..."
try
	while Base.isopen(server)
		sleep(.1)
	end
catch e
	@info "Closing server..."
	close(server)
	wait(server)
end

```

> Which versions (if any?) of a package (by name) can be installed from a reachable registry?

Use cases in https://github.com/fonsp/Pluto.jl/pull/844 include (screenshots attached):
- [autocomplete all available packages when typing `using Plo<TAB>`](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6933510/121342763-434b2000-c922-11eb-8c8d-7e7a447b5581.png)
- [show that a package name is not found in the registry](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6933510/121342888-65dd3900-c922-11eb-956b-05a629b491c3.png)
- [show which version will be installed]() (though this also requires https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/11)
- show whether a package has a more recent version than the one installed

# Current implementation

I use internal API to find all registries, then internal `Pkg.Types.read_registry` to parse it, and then internal API to iterate and query the entries matching a package name.

# Difference with https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/11

is what was decided in https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/2639
cc. @st-- @fredrikekre 

```
% ../julia/julia --project --depwarn=yes
...
julia> import Pkg
[ Info: Precompiling Pkg [44cfe95a-1eb2-52ea-b672-e2afdf69b79f]

julia> Pkg.activate("/tmp/123") do
       Pkg.status()
       end
┌ Warning: `activate(f::Function, new_project::AbstractString)` is deprecated, use `Operations.activate(f::Function, new_project::AbstractString)` instead.
│   caller = top-level scope at REPL[2]:1
└ @ Core REPL[2]:1
Status `/private/tmp/123/Project.toml` (empty project)
```

But does it achieve the right message? It's not saying "don't use this", just "use this other function".
Perhaps `@deprecate` needs a `warn_against = true` mode that says something like:

```
┌ Warning: `activate(f::Function, new_project::AbstractString)` is deprecated, this functionality should not be used but  `Operations.activate(f::Function, new_project::AbstractString)` does exist as a replacement.
│   caller = top-level scope at REPL[2]:1
└ @ Core REPL[2]:1

I believe this is very useful, and our particular use case is the Pluto server process, which maintains multiple project environments in parallel.

The argument for removing this behaviour is that [changing environments dynamically is not recommended](https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/2639#issuecomment-865965532). But then why do we have `Pkg.activate(::String)`?

Ideally, I would like `Pkg.Types.Context` to be public API. (In internal Pkg API, `Context` is always the first argument to any function, [e.g. `Pkg.API.add(context, ["Plots", "Test"])`](https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/blob/16c3d6a7ad0075a56c8da300811b0e5249ab9e39/src/API.jl#L226).) This is easy API to manage multiple environments, and it does not modify `Base.LOAD_PATH`.

cc. @st-- @fredrikekre 

```
% ../julia/julia --project --depwarn=yes
...
julia> import Pkg
[ Info: Precompiling Pkg [44cfe95a-1eb2-52ea-b672-e2afdf69b79f]

julia> Pkg.activate("/tmp/123") do
       Pkg.status()
       end
┌ Warning: `activate(f::Function, new_project::AbstractString)` is deprecated, use `Operations.activate(f::Function, new_project::AbstractString)` instead.
│   caller = top-level scope at REPL[2]:1
└ @ Core REPL[2]:1
Status `/private/tmp/123/Project.toml` (empty project)
```

But does it achieve the right message? It's not saying "don't use this", just "use this other function".
Perhaps `@deprecate` needs a `warn_against = true` mode that says something like:

```
┌ Warning: `activate(f::Function, new_project::AbstractString)` is deprecated, this functionality should not be used but  `Operations.activate(f::Function, new_project::AbstractString)` does exist as a replacement.
│   caller = top-level scope at REPL[2]:1
└ @ Core REPL[2]:1
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/2912/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/2912/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/1123396789","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/2912#issuecomment-1123396789","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/2912","id":1123396789,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBOhtSc5C9ay1","user":{"login":"fonsp","id":6933510,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY5MzM1MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6933510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp","html_url":"https://github.com/fonsp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-05-11T09:03:29Z","updated_at":"2022-05-11T09:03:29Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Please don't remove `Pkg.activate`! 👀 That's not what I meant at all","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/1123396789/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

It [then used](https://github.com/fonsp/Pluto.jl/blob/16bba29e0fa488f139698e7ac13fb3d67ec81bd6/src/runner/PlutoRunner.jl#L1430) `Tables.rows(sol)` to get the data-to-be-displayed, which is implemented in SciMLBase: 
https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/a34f5a71604d0d3207fa54f53e0da1dbbb89cd9d/src/tabletraits.jl#L18-L29 

This method accesses `sol.t`, which throws an error for `sol::OptimizationSolution`.

If the table-like trait was not intentional, you could add:
```julia
Tables.isrowtable(::Type{<:AbstractOptimizationSolution}) = false
```

But I am also fixing this on the Pluto side: https://github.com/fonsp/Pluto.jl/pull/2109 It seems that the array/table interface is not reliable to use "in the wild", so we are going to add a `text/plain` fallback, which would have prevented this issue.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/issues/comments/1132639104/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

This PR makes it possible to decode base64-encoded strings/streams without padding 🎉, matching [the behaviour in V8 and in the browser (in data urls)](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6933510/157058942-53538eed-af03-4f11-8447-82a00bb14227.png).

I believe that sentence is about pad *bits*, not the padding character `=`. For example, base64-encoding the single-byte input 0b11111111 becomes
```
First     Second    Third    Fourth
   /
111111    11xxxx    yyyyyy   yyyyyy       
```

Here, `x` refers to pad bits, `y` to pad characters.

The first character should be `/`, but the second character depends on the choice of padding bits (`x`). The spec states that during encoding, these should be zero (thus giving `110000`, which is `w`), but during decoding, pad bits should be ignored, so any character between `110000` and `111111` should lead to the same result.

Although this was not part of my PR (and it was already implemented correctly), I added some tests about decoding pad *bits* here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44503/files#diff-4fcfc93f2fff4705dceba7d3c1ef7d3aa0c7653ccff897ddc7ab664c4f9b1e37R102 , and cross-checked these with Chrome's implementation. ```
I expected `.` to match *any* character, including invalid characters.

In JS (V8 engine), which also allows arbitrary data in strings, the result is:
```js
> let c = new TextDecoder().decode(new Uint8Array([0x80]))

> /.+/.exec(`hello${c}world`)
[ "hello�world" ]
```

```
> const url = "data:;base64,aGVsbG8="
undefined
> await (await fetch(url, {method: "GET"})).text()
"hello"
> await (await fetch(url, {method: "HEAD"})).text()
""
> await (await fetch(url, {method: "PUT"})).text()
"hello"
> await (await fetch(url, {method: "POST"})).text()
"hello"
> await (await fetch(url, {method: "POST", body: "world"})).text()
"hello"
```

Looking at the [spec](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org), it looks like [nothing is returned for `HEAD` or `CONNECT` (§4.1.19)](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#:~:text=request%E2%80%99s%20method%20is%20%60-,HEAD,-%60%20or%20%60CONNECT%60%2C%20or). {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Downloads.jl/issues/181","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Downloads.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Downloads.jl/issues/181/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Downloads.jl/issues/181/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Downloads.jl/issues/181/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Downloads.jl/issues/181","id":1163184988,"node_id":"I_kwDOESJ56s5FVMtc","number":181,"title":"Read output stream after reading response headers","user":{"login":"fonsp","id":6933510,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY5MzM1MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6933510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp","html_url":"https://github.com/fonsp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-03-08T22:00:18Z","updated_at":"2022-03-08T22:00:18Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Some `request` implementations in other languages work slightly differently: they *first* return a `Response` containing HTTP status and headers, after which the user initiates reading. ## [`fetch`](https://javascript.info/fetch) in JS ## [`requests`](https://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/) in Python3 What this enables is: - you can pipe the IO into a JSON/CSV/JPEG/MP3/etc parser, depending on the `Content-Type` header of the response. - you can choose not to read the response body depending on the headers (I'm thinking of cache headers) I also think this is a "feature" of HTTP: HTTP status and headers are received first, describing how to remaining body should be read. --- Is this behaviour already possible with `Downloads.request`? I have the impression that right now, `request` will *first* write the full body content into the given `output::IO`, and *then* it returns the `Response` object containing headers and such. Thanks for the awesome package!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Downloads.jl/issues/181/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Downloads.jl/issues/181/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null}}

noticed this behaviour on 1.7.0 (it throws an error on 1.6.5, see #25997 for prior discussion):
```julia
julia> match(r".+", "hello\x80world")
RegexMatch("hello")
```
I expected `.` to match *any* character, including invalid characters.

In JS (V8 engine), which also allows arbitrary data in strings, the result is:
```js
> let c = new TextDecoder().decode(new Uint8Array([0x80]))

> /.+/.exec(`hello${c}world`)
[ "hello�world" ]
```

will help new Julia programmers who don't know about `JULIA_NUM_THREADS=auto` get fast execution. It also symbolizes how Julia supports the increasing parallelism of modern technical computing. Anybody who needs sequential execution, e.g. for debugging, can use `JULIA_NUM_THREADS=1`.

- [Rust's Rayon](https://github.com/rayon-rs/rayon/blob/master/FAQ.md#how-many-threads-will-rayon-spawn) uses the number of logical cores by default.

- [Intel® Cilk™ Plus](http://wwweic.eri.u-tokyo.ac.jp/computer/manual/eic2015/doc/intel/en_US/compiler_c/GUID-85B8EB6D-A49C-4D44-B58E-88DCA85FBE23.htm) uses the number of hardware threads.

- [Crystal](https://crystal-lang.org/2019/09/06/parallelism-in-crystal.html) defaults to 4.

- [Fortran OMP](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19205-01/819-5262/aeuip/index.html) defaults to 1.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43672/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":4,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43672/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1074426243","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43672#issuecomment-1074426243","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43672","id":1074426243,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5ACnGD","user":{"login":"fonsp","id":6933510,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY5MzM1MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6933510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp","html_url":"https://github.com/fonsp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-21T21:15:17Z","updated_at":"2022-03-21T21:15:17Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"We could use `min(1, Sys.CPU_THREADS ÷ 2)` as a safe default while waiting on that issue? I was expecting there to be an optional keyword argument for `readdir` to omit hidden files and directories. Currently I'm doing this

```julia
function non_hidden_files(dir; kwargs...)
    files = readdir(dir; kwargs...)
    filter(!startswith(".") ∘ basename, files)
end
```

which works for hidden files but not for hidden directories. It seems like it would be handy to have an `omit_hidden` or similar option for `readdir`.

I encountered this because of the pesky `.DS_Store` files on MacOS. :joy:","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38841/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38841/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1048645708","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38841#issuecomment-1048645708","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/38841","id":1048645708,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM4-gRBM","user":{"login":"fonsp","id":6933510,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY5MzM1MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6933510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp","html_url":"https://github.com/fonsp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-02-23T10:35:05Z","updated_at":"2022-02-23T10:35:05Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"This would be great for UI applications written in Julia :)

`ends_with_semicolon`","user":{"login":"fonsp","id":6933510,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY5MzM1MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6933510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp","html_url":"https://github.com/fonsp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":10,"created_at":"2021-12-09T00:11:49Z","updated_at":"2022-01-14T21:46:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/43374","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43374","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43374.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43374.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"`REPL.ends_with_semicolon` is used to determine whether or not to display output in the REPL, but IJulia and Pluto also use it. This PR uses some regex replacements instead of a stateful search for a more accurate heuristic. 

I did read and agree with [this comment](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/28743#issuecomment-504781166), but it turned out to be fairly easy to implement a "parser" good enough for this purpose. The new implementation is actually shorter, albeit with more "regex magic".

The new strategy requires a bit more calculation, but I think that this problem is not performance sensitive, and accuracy is more important? Let me know if you agree.

Just a small comment: In the Julia downloads page, `x86` refers to the whole family, including 32-bit and 64-bit. On an ARM-based computer (like the new macs), I would expect `juliaup add 1.7.1` to install the ARM 64-bit build, and `juliaup add 1.6.1~x86` would install the x86 64-bit build, not the 32-bit build. On the README: it states: > `juliaup add 1.6.1~x86` installs the 32 bit version of Julia 1.6.1 on your system. fixes #41440, #43285 and similar runtime crashes, which stem from `CodeModel::Large` not being correctly implemented on MachO/ARM64.

With it, the only testsuite failures remaining on my M1 Max machine are occasional thread.jl hangs, which appear to be the same as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/41820.

Now that master is on LLVM 13, this should work against the default BinaryBuilder LLVM. LLVM 14 (Git main) is still preferred, as debugger integration (`ENABLE_GDBLISTENER`) is not supported on 13.x. I tested https://github.com/dnadlinger/llvm-project/commit/57eb8c10025bdbca8f8260bd43a46d0aca369fe8, which is llvm/llvm-project@1dd5e6fed5db with our 13.x patches. Our LLVM patch set (julia-releases/13.x) is absolutely required so that `__eh_frame` unwind information is generated and passed through the stack, without which backtraces silently won't work (see llvm/llvm-project#52921).

Profiler integration (`ENABLE_JITPROFILING`) is not currently supported on either version; this is an upstream LLVM restriction.

---

As pointed out in the comments, this currently doesn't use the custom Julia RTDyld memory manager, as the API has changed quite significantly. We'll probably want to port the dual-map optimisations to JITLinkMemoryManager soon, but I believe the random segfaults are much more disruptive, and thus the JITLink switch shouldn't be held up by that.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43664/reactions","total_count":29,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":19,"confused":0,"heart":10,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43664/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1023697451","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43664#issuecomment-1023697451","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43664","id":1023697451,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM49BGIr","user":{"login":"fonsp","id":6933510,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY5MzM1MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6933510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp","html_url":"https://github.com/fonsp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-01-27T22:23:34Z","updated_at":"2022-01-27T22:23:34Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"It looks like this PR fixed many segfault issues for M1 users (in CSV, Pkg, MLJ), yayy!
